archetypes.name: The Vibe Coder
archetypes.description: Individual developers using casual AI prompting to rapidly prototype and build applications. They describe what they want in natural language and accept AI output with minimal review, optimizing for speed over code quality. Term coined by Andrej Karpathy in February 2025.
key_patterns: Natural language descriptions instead of code
Accept AI output with minimal review
friction_points: AI slop: code that looks reasonable but lacks error handling, security, maintainability
95% feel productive while measurably producing lower-quality code
41% higher code churn
63% have spent more time debugging AI code than writing it themselves
examples: Scientific American coverage of non-technical users building with Claude Code

archetypes.name: The Plan-Driven Architect
archetypes.description: Experienced engineers who enforce a strict research-plan-implement methodology. They never let Claude write code until a written plan has been reviewed and approved, use annotation cycles to inject domain constraints, and treat Claude as a capable junior that needs architectural direction.
archetypes.typical_domain: full-stack web development, platform engineering, large codebases
key_patterns: Research phase with persistent research.md artifacts
Plan.md with approach, code snippets, file paths, trade-offs
Reference-based corrections pointing to existing code patterns
friction_points: Context window filling up degrades performance
Claude doesn't know product priorities without annotation
Auto-compaction is opaque and error-prone
examples: Boris Tane (Engineering Lead, Cloudflare, founder of Baselime)
Boris Cherny (Creator of Claude Code at Anthropic)

archetypes.name: The Parallel Orchestrator
archetypes.description: Power users who run 5-15 Claude Code instances simultaneously, leveraging git worktrees for isolation and treating sessions like a distributed team of junior developers. They optimize for throughput by eliminating idle time and use system notifications to context-switch between sessions.
archetypes.typical_domain: platform engineering, multi-feature development, team leads
archetypes.workflow_style: autonomous with supervisory oversight, massively parallel
key_patterns: Opus with thinking for all tasks (quality over speed)
Leaderboards tracking team Claude Code usage
friction_points: Token costs scale linearly with teammate count
Split panes require tmux/iTerm2 (incompatible with VS Code)
No session resumption for in-process teammates
Rate limits hit faster with parallel usage
examples: Patrick Mineault (5-10 Claude Code windows in parallel)

archetypes.name: The Infrastructure Builder
archetypes.description: Users who treat Claude Code as an operating system rather than a coding assistant. They build extensive skill libraries, hook systems, MCP server integrations, and persistent workspaces that create an autonomous development environment running 24/7 with scheduled tasks and cross-tool integration.
archetypes.typical_domain: devops, platform engineering, autonomous workflows, content automation
key_patterns: Pre/post hooks for safety and automation
8+ MCP server integrations (Postgres, GitHub, Figma, Slack, etc.)
Voice note to agent processing pipelines
friction_points: MCP server uptime management
Local container management complexity
Cross-device sync challenges
examples: Jan Luca Sandmann (computer-agents.com, 12 skills, 8 MCP servers)
Daniil Okhlopkov (Head of Analytics, TON Foundation, Coolify+Telegram+Codex MCPs)

archetypes.name: The Enterprise Team Lead
archetypes.description: Engineering leads who manage shared CLAUDE.md files across teams, enforce quality gates via hooks and CI, and use Claude Code's analytics dashboard to track adoption and productivity. They focus on governance, reproducibility, and compounding institutional knowledge.
archetypes.typical_domain: enterprise SaaS, large engineering teams, platform companies
archetypes.workflow_style: plan-driven with team governance, supervised autonomy
key_patterns: Shared CLAUDE.md checked into git, updated multiple times weekly
AGENTS.md maintained in sync for cross-IDE compatibility
Pre-commit hooks blocking credential file commits
PR review prompts customized for bugs/vulnerabilities over style
Analytics dashboards tracking lines accepted, PRs shipped, sessions
friction_points: 30%+ of engineering team must use a tool before it goes in CLAUDE.md
Token budget management across teams
Balancing CLAUDE.md size (under 300 lines) with completeness
LLM instruction-following degrades uniformly as instruction count increases
examples: Shrivu Shankar (Abnormal AI, 13KB CLAUDE.md for monorepo)
Anthropic internal teams (132 engineers surveyed, 200K transcripts analyzed)

archetypes.name: The Research Scientist
archetypes.description: Academic researchers who use Claude Code for the scientific loop: data acquisition, processing, analysis, and visualization. They emphasize verification through plots and tests, advocate against pure autonomy in favor of collaborative steering, and structure projects with clear data lineage.
archetypes.typical_domain: ML research, computational science, academic labs
key_patterns: Heavy use of Esc to interrupt and redirect Claude
Shift+Tab for plan iteration before execution
Generate lots of plots for cheap validation
friction_points: Tools can produce wrong results faster than ever
Junior researchers lack metacognition to identify errors
Requires prior coding experience for effective use
Frontier research may need narrow focus incompatible with parallel sessions
examples: Pedro Sant'Anna (Emory University, 6 PhD lecture decks with 800+ slides via Claude Code)
2.1% of scientists with ORCID-linked GitHub profiles use Claude Code (Feb 2026)

archetypes.name: The DevOps Automator
archetypes.description: Infrastructure engineers who use Claude Code for IaC generation, Kubernetes troubleshooting, cost optimization, and CI/CD pipeline automation. They leverage headless mode for non-interactive automation and specialized skills for Terraform, Pulumi, and cloud platforms.
archetypes.typical_domain: devops, SRE, cloud infrastructure, server management
archetypes.workflow_style: autonomous via headless mode, CI/CD integrated
key_patterns: 60%+ of adopting teams use non-interactive mode
Changelog generation, code migration, accessibility audits
friction_points: Safety concerns with autonomous infrastructure changes
Permission management in headless mode
Requires --dangerously-skip-permissions for MCP server mode
examples: Pulumi (maintains skills repository for IaC patterns)
Fluid (open-source tool positioned as Claude Code for Infrastructure)
45% code review time reduction reported on 50K+ line projects

archetypes.name: The Knowledge Architect
archetypes.description: Users who build multi-layered knowledge systems around Claude Code with persistent memory graphs, codebase graph databases, hierarchical navigation manifests, and cross-project propagation rules. They treat the development environment as a queryable knowledge base that compounds over time.
archetypes.typical_domain: ML research, multi-project management, academic/industry hybrid
key_patterns: 3-layer knowledge architecture (code structure, data flow, learnings)
6+ MCP servers for different knowledge domains
7+ hooks for safety, formatting, context awareness
Graph databases for code intelligence (KuzuDB, GitNexus)
friction_points: High initial setup investment
Maintaining consistency across 6+ projects
Context window pressure from large instruction sets
17% friction rate (buggy code + wrong approach incidents)
examples: Robert Frenken (author's own setup: 6 projects, 3 knowledge layers, 171 sessions in 16 days)

dimensions.name: Session Intensity
dimensions.description: How many sessions per day and hours per day a user spends in Claude Code
dimensions.scale: low (1-2 sessions/day, <1hr) to extreme (10+ sessions/day, 8+ hours)
data_points: Average cost $6/dev/day, 90th percentile under $12/day
Pro users: 10-40 prompts per 5-hour window
Max 20x users: 200-800 prompts per window
Boris Cherny: 5 local + 5-10 web sessions simultaneously
Robert Frenken: 10.7 sessions/day over 16 days, 179 total hours
Usage resets every ~5 hours
50 sessions/month triggers throttling (1.67/day average)

dimensions.name: Tooling Depth
dimensions.description: How much infrastructure a user builds around Claude Code (CLAUDE.md, hooks, skills, MCP servers, custom scripts)
dimensions.scale: minimal (default config) to infrastructure (custom knowledge graphs, multi-layer systems, 10+ integrations)
data_points: Boris Cherny's team CLAUDE.md: 2.5k tokens
Shrivu Shankar's monorepo CLAUDE.md: 13KB with potential for 25KB
Fresh monorepo session costs ~20k tokens baseline (10% of 200k window)
Jan Luca Sandmann: 12 skills, 8 MCP servers
Daniil Okhlopkov: 3 MCP servers, 3 custom skills (~200 lines each)
Robert Frenken: 7 hooks, 15 skills, 6 MCP servers, 3 knowledge layers
LLMs can follow ~150-200 instructions reliably; Claude system prompt already uses ~50

dimensions.name: Autonomy Level
dimensions.description: How much human supervision vs autonomous agent execution characterizes the workflow
dimensions.scale: fully interactive (every edit reviewed) to fully autonomous (headless CI/CD, scheduled agents)
data_points: Over 50% of respondents report only 0-20% of work can be fully delegated (Anthropic internal)
60%+ of teams use non-interactive mode for automation
Claude Code max consecutive actions increased from ~10 to ~21 (116% increase over 6 months)
Human intervention turns decreased 33% (from 6.2 to 4.1 per transcript)
83.8% of agent-assisted PRs accepted; 54.9% merged without modification
Code can only be relied upon ~30% of the time on first try (community report)

dimensions.name: Planning Rigor
dimensions.description: How structured and deliberate the user's approach is before letting Claude generate code
dimensions.scale: ad-hoc (describe and accept) to spec-driven (research.md, plan.md, annotation cycles, quality gates)
data_points: Boris Tane: 1-6 annotation cycles before implementation
90% of sessions recommended to start in plan mode
Spec-driven development emerged as counter to vibe coding failures
PEV Loop (Plan-Execute-Verify) identified as core agentic methodology
Shrivu Shankar: 'Don't trust auto-compaction. Use /clear for simple reboots.'

dimensions.name: Parallelism
dimensions.description: How many concurrent Claude Code sessions or agents a user manages simultaneously
dimensions.scale: single session to 15+ parallel instances with agent teams
data_points: Boris Cherny: 5 local + 5-10 web = up to 15 simultaneous
Patrick Mineault: 5-10 parallel windows
incident.io team: 4-5 simultaneous agents
Agent teams: experimental feature with shared task list, inbox messaging, file ownership boundaries
9 parallel subagents for code review (HAMY blog)
10-20% session abandonment rate for parallel work

dimensions.name: Verification Discipline
dimensions.description: How rigorously users verify Claude's output before accepting it
dimensions.scale: trust-and-ship to multi-gate verification (tests, screenshots, CI, adversarial review)
data_points: Boris Cherny: verification feedback loops improve results 2-3x
Shrivu Shankar: block-at-submit hooks force test-and-fix loop until green
Patrick Mineault: lots of plots for cheap validation
45% say debugging AI code takes longer than writing manually
66% cite 'almost correct but not quite' as biggest frustration
AI-generated code has ~1.7x more defects, up to 2.7x more security vulnerabilities

sessions_per_day.notes: Low based on 50 sessions/month throttle threshold. High based on Boris Cherny's parallel local sessions. Power user is Robert Frenken's measured rate over 16 days. No industry-wide median published.

commits_per_session.notes: No public per-session commit data found. Claude Code produces 135,000+ commits/day on GitHub (4% of all public commits as of Feb 2026). Robert Frenken: 0.6 commits/session over 16 days.

tooling_layers.description: Typical users rely on default CLAUDE.md and built-in features. Power users add 2-5 custom slash commands, 1-3 hooks, and 1-2 MCP servers. Infrastructure builders create 10+ skills, 5+ hooks, 5+ MCP servers, and custom knowledge graph databases.
tooling_layers.typical_user: Default config, <60 line CLAUDE.md, 0-2 slash commands
tooling_layers.power_user: 2-5 slash commands, 1-3 hooks, 1-3 MCP servers, CLAUDE.md <300 lines
tooling_layers.infrastructure_builder: 10+ skills, 5+ hooks, 5+ MCP servers, knowledge graphs, custom scripts, 3+ knowledge layers

adoption_rates: 92.6% of developers use an AI coding assistant at least monthly (DX Insight, 51K+ devs)
85% use AI regularly (JetBrains 2025, 24,534 devs across 194 countries)
84% use or plan to use AI tools (Stack Overflow, 49K+ responses)
51% of professional developers use AI tools daily
91% of engineering organizations have adopted at least one AI coding tool
90% of Fortune 100 companies use GitHub Copilot
23% of developers regularly use AI agents (Stack Overflow 2025)
38% have no plans to adopt agents
2.1% of scientists with ORCID-linked GitHub profiles use Claude Code (Feb 2026, projected ~10% by end 2026)
GitHub Copilot: 20M+ users, 4.7M paid subscribers (2026, +75% YoY)

productivity_claims.source: METR study (16 experienced devs, 246 tasks, avg 2hr each)
productivity_claims.note: Developers predicted 24% speedup, believed 20% speedup post-study

productivity_claims.claim: +50% self-reported productivity boost (up from +20% one year prior)
67% increase in merged PRs per engineer daily
+21% tasks, +98% PRs merged, +154% PR size, +91% review time, +9% bugs, ZERO org-level gains
productivity_claims.source: Faros AI Paradox Report (10,000+ devs, 1,255 teams)

productivity_claims.claim: ~3.6 hours/week saved on average (daily users: ~4.1 hrs)
Features shipped in 30-60 minutes instead of 8-10 hours
productivity_claims.source: Full-stack developer using Wasp framework + Claude Code

productivity_claims.claim: Complex blockchain research reduced from full work week to one evening

code_quality: Only ~30% of AI-suggested code gets accepted (Copilot acceptance rate)
83.8% of Claude Code agent-assisted PRs accepted/merged (567 PRs, 157 repos)
54.9% merged without modification; 45.1% needed human revision
Up to 2.7x more security vulnerabilities in AI-generated code
48% of AI-generated code contains security vulnerabilities
Only 33% fully trust AI-generated code; 3% highly trust
Code reliable ~30% of first try (community report)

token_usage.typical_session: 10,000-50,000 tokens for simple tasks; 50K-200K for medium sessions
token_usage.cache_ratio: 90%+ of all tokens are cache reads; input/output under 1% combined; cache writes ~6%
token_usage.claude_code_efficiency: 5.5x fewer tokens than Cursor for identical tasks

github_impact.ai_tools_code_share_2026: 41% of all code written by AI tools

anthropic_internal_data.source: Anthropic internal research (132 engineers, 53 interviews, 200K transcripts, Aug 2025)
anthropic_internal_data.new_work_enabled: 27% of Claude-assisted tasks represent entirely new work
anthropic_internal_data.undesirable_tasks_offloaded: 44% of Claude-assisted work are tasks employees wouldn't prefer doing manually
anthropic_internal_data.delegation_capacity: Over 50% report only 0-20% of work can be fully delegated
anthropic_internal_data.colleague_replacement: Some report 80-90% of questions now go to Claude rather than colleagues

named_power_users.name: Boris Cherny
named_power_users.role: Creator/Head of Claude Code at Anthropic
key_practices: 100% of code written by AI since Nov 2025
Team CLAUDE.md at 2.5k tokens, updated multiple times weekly

named_power_users.name: Boris Tane
key_practices: Single long sessions with context window compaction

named_power_users.name: Shrivu Shankar
key_practices: Only documents tools used by 30%+ of engineers
Migrated most MCPs to simple CLIs

named_power_users.name: Steve Sewell
key_practices: Claude Code as primary interface (not secondary)
VS Code extension for parallel instances
Customized PR review prompt for bugs over style

named_power_users.name: Daniil Okhlopkov
key_practices: Voice notes to agent processing pipeline

named_power_users.name: Patrick Mineault
key_practices: Esc with abandon for mid-conversation steering
marimo for notebooks, Snakemake for DAGs

named_power_users.name: Jan Luca Sandmann
key_practices: 8 MCP servers (Postgres, GitHub, Figma, Slack, etc.)
Permanent worker agents (Research Bot, Code Reviewer, Personal Assistant)
3 side projects built/deployed hands-off in 30 days

named_power_users.name: Addy Osmani
key_practices: Agent teams for competing debugging hypotheses
Parallel code review (security, performance, coverage separately)
Cross-layer feature work (frontend, backend, testing agents)
File ownership boundaries to prevent merge conflicts
Delegate mode restricting leads to coordination-only

key_paradoxes.name: Perception vs Reality Gap
key_paradoxes.description: Developers believe they are 20% faster; METR controlled measurement shows 19% slower

key_paradoxes.name: Individual vs Organizational Gap
key_paradoxes.description: Individual devs produce +21% more tasks and +98% more PRs, but zero measurable organization-level improvement. Downstream bottlenecks absorb gains (Amdahl's Law).
key_paradoxes.source: Faros AI Paradox Report (10,000+ devs, 1,255 teams)

key_paradoxes.name: Speed vs Quality Gap
key_paradoxes.description: 74% report productivity gains and 95% feel productive, but code has 1.7x more defects and 41% higher churn
key_paradoxes.source: Multiple surveys aggregated by Panto AI

key_paradoxes.name: Adoption vs Trust Gap
key_paradoxes.description: 85-93% adopt AI tools, but only 33% trust the output. 46% actively distrust.

robert_profile.archetype_mapping: Knowledge Architect with Parallel Orchestrator tendencies
distinguishing_features: 3-layer knowledge architecture (GitNexus code graph + SQLite data flow + memory MCP) is unique among documented users
HPC/SLURM integration is rare in Claude Code community (most users are web/SaaS)
6 projects managed simultaneously with cross-repo propagation triggers
Ownership registry enforcing single-source-of-truth across 20+ rules files
Hierarchical manifest-based navigation with health scoring and smell detection
10.7 sessions/day exceeds even Boris Cherny's documented parallel usage
